title: Mixup: Blending Two Images
concept: Mixup is a simple multi-image augmentation technique. It takes two random images and blends their pixel values using a weighted average, while blending their labels in the exact same proportion.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What do we do to the target labels when applying multi-image augmentation?

options.text: We blend them proportionally to match the mix ratio of the input images
We keep the label of the first image at 100% and discard the second
We change the labels to random numbers

exercises.explain: Yes! Blending labels proportionally is key to making the technique work.

cards.title: Double Exposure
cards.body: Imagine taking a photo on old film, but forgetting to wind the roll. You snap a photo of a cat, then a photo of a dog on top of it. You get a transparent, ghost-like overlay of both animals. This is the exact idea behind Mixup.

cards.title: What is Mixup?
cards.body:
Mixup is a technique that blends two images by calculating a weighted average of their pixel values. If we select a mixing factor (lambda) of 0.7, the new image is:
Mixup Image = (0.7 × Image A) + (0.3 × Image B).

cards.title: Mixing paint
cards.body: Mixup is like mixing paint. If you stir 70% blue paint and 30% yellow paint together, you get a new shade of green. You don't get distinct blue and yellow spots — the colors blend completely at every point on the canvas.

cards.title: A Cat-Dog Blend
cards.body: If Image A is a Cat and Image B is a Dog, and we use a 70% blend factor, the model is shown the overlaid image and taught: 'This image is 70% Cat and 30% Dog.' The model's loss function evaluates how close its predictions are to this exact 0.7/0.3 split.

cards.title: It looks weird, but it works
cards.body: To a human, Mixup images look like confusing, transparent ghosts. You might think this would ruin a model's ability to see clear shapes. However, mathematically, it acts as a regularizer, preventing the network from drawing sharp, oversensitive boundary lines in its feature space.

cards.title: Mixup Pixel & Label Math
cards.body: How Mixup applies a weighted average to both inputs:

left.text:
Image A: Cat
Label A: [1.0 Cat, 0.0 Dog]
Weight: 0.7

right.text:
Image B: Dog
Label B: [0.0 Cat, 1.0 Dog]
Weight: 0.3

exercises.prompt: If Mixup combines a plane image (A) and a bird image (B) with a weight of 0.8 on image A, what is the target label?
exercises.explain: Correct! The label blend matches the input pixel blend ratio (0.8 and 0.2).

exercises.prompt: True or false: Mixup modifies only the images, leaving the target labels at 100% for the dominant class.
exercises.explain: False. Mixup blends both the image pixels AND the labels in the exact same proportion.

exercises.sentence: Mixup blends images by calculating a weighted ___ of their pixel values.
exercises.explain: Mixup uses a weighted average (linear combination) of the pixel values.

exercises.prompt: What does a Mixup image look like to a human?

options.text: A semi-transparent overlay of two different images on top of each other
A sharp collage where one image is cropped and pasted into the center of another
A perfectly normal image with no visible modifications

exercises.explain: Correct! It looks like a semi-transparent double exposure.

exercises.prompt: If we apply Mixup to a batch of images, what mathematical effect does it have on the model's decision boundary?

options.text: It linearizes the transitions between classes, forcing the model to change its predictions smoothly rather than making sharp jumps
It forces the model's filters to only detect horizontal lines
It completely removes all negative values from the weights
It has no mathematical effect; it is purely a visual compression technique

exercises.explain: Excellent! Mixup linearizes class transitions, preventing overfitting. Next, let's explore CutMix, a patch-based approach!
